name: By what authority
notes: A threefold synoptic account: the chief priests, scribes, and elders confront Jesus in the temple, demanding by what authority he does these things and who gave it to him. He answers with a counter-question about the baptism of John, whence it was, from heaven or of men, and their private reasoning traps them, for to say from heaven indicts their unbelief, while to say of men provokes the people who hold John a prophet. Unable to answer, they plead they cannot tell, and so he declines to tell them by what authority he acts. The accounts share this core almost word for word; each keeps a complementary vantage, Mark and Luke noting the scribes among the questioners and that they came again to Jerusalem or upon him as he preached the gospel, Matthew compressing the same scene, and Luke alone sounding the sharper fear that the people will stone them. The slight differences in wording are complementary, the threefold witness of one event.
